speaker: interviewer
text: --Nelken, 24, November, 1995. We're continuing now with--

speaker: interviewee
text: Alexander Weissberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: --critical qualities on Alexander Weissman, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he was arrested during the big purges by Stalin, he-- every Nobel Prize winner, Joliot-Curie, the daughter and his wife, and [INAUDIBLE], and including Albert Einstein, they wrote to Stalin about Alexander Weissberg that this must be a mistake, that he absolutely could not be involved in killing Stalin or Molotov or whatever. But of course, it was no answer whatsoever. However, he was so every day in this inquiry by-- by the KBG and so forth for two years or more. And then-- but nothing ever happened. And they keep him and exchange him with some other German and some other Jews who were there. [INAUDIBLE] they give them into the German hands at the beginning of the war, because they were sure that they will kill them, the German will kill them all. But Weissberg run away from the transport in Poland. And he came into the Jewish-- into the Krakow ghetto. And there, this is how he met me. He was in-- in that Polish Underground. Being a physicist, he was preparing all kind of, you know, these things to spraying, the bombs and so forth. And then he wanted badly take me out of the  ghetto and, you know, in order to save me. But I would never part with my family. So I stay in ghetto. And he left. He just disappear. When he disappear, he went and was-- first he was fighting later on. Because this was also '42, in Jewish, well, uprising in Warsaw in the ghetto, and then in Polish uprising in ghetto in-- not in ghetto, but in Warsaw. And later on, he was saved by a Polish aristocratic woman with whom he later lived in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was  her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cybulski, Sophia Cybulski, C-Y-B-U-L-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: C or Z?

speaker: interviewee
text: C.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: C, like Cecilia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that  she saved him and where?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war, he was-- she fell in love with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did  she save him?

speaker: interviewee
text: The big house. He was preparing all these bombs in the her apartment or in her other places. And, you know, as soon he-- the war was over, he wrote these books which are in foreign languages-- [GERMAN] in German, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], and then and all-- in all languages. And Churchill can see that he spoke the greatest for-- for all this iron-- well, this-- that-- that he make this word Iron Curtain. And this was a great truth. He was the very first one to open the [? lights ?] of communism and of Russia right after the war, in about '48 or so. And it took so many years till the world finally find out what is it. But anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you're saying Churchill derived the term Iron Curtain from--

speaker: interviewee
text: From him.

speaker: interviewer
text: --this man's work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Alexander Weissberg [INAUDIBLE]. And then, well, in-- in that introduction to his books was written by this-- I forgot his name, who wrote, well, the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And everybody knew what was going on with the Gulags and everything. And I think, a-ha, this was his. And in his book, first I start looking if this is him. And I really found many places very similar, his way of speaking and this, this, this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, this was my great experience, very innocent. And he always said, everybody would laugh completely knowing how innocent we spent time together. But I was enjoying it tremendously.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so did he. And he-- half of my diary is full of him. I was quoting him by word by word.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- and who was that? We mentioned her name. But who was the woman who saved him? Who was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a wife of a Polish film actor, a very nice Polish woman, who fell deadly-- her husband was killed. And she fell deadly in love with him. And this is it. But now everybody's dead. Not only Alexander Weissberg, who die about 15 years ago, his wife, and even that woman who gave me --

speaker: interviewer
text: Who  saved him.

speaker: interviewee
text: --his photograph.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto, did you know the truth about the extermination camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We even did not know, at least I did not know, about the extermination camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you first find out about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a rumor that there is going to be a concentration camp for us in Plaszow. But about Auschwitz, I did not know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you never knew the meaning of what it--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I thought it will be like an arbeitslager. Well, that this is not concentration camp. But as a matter of fact--

speaker: interviewer
text: Something like Fliegerhorst, you're saying.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, something like that. Fliegerhorst, as a matter of fact, was not that bad. Because this was Luftwaffe. They were-- this was Air Force. And they were not Gestapo. And they were-- they were afraid of Gestapo as we were and of SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I don't know. I think, because this is it. Everybody was afraid of SS were.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the dynamic?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Air Force, they were soldiers. They were flyers. They were fighting. They were on the front. And they did not deal too much with politics. As a matter of fact, very often the soldiers--

speaker: interviewer
text: But why were they are afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reason for the fear that everyone--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because everybody in Germany was afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it psychological or was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Everybody in Germany was afraid of anybody else. Even the SS, when he was one so, and watching us, it was not so bad. When there were only two, right away they start yelling [INAUDIBLE]. Because they were afraid of one another.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when one was alone, you noticed a very different personality often.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not with everyone. Who was SOB was SOB all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, but sometimes they were just simply-- they always, for example, told us, when somebody else come, move, move, move, move, move. Don't-- don't just stand.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- the diary that you have referenced a number of times throughout this interview was something that you prepared during wartime and subsequent to this period. What-- what portions were prepared during this-- the-- the war period--

speaker: interviewee
text: The diary--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and the pre-war period?

speaker: interviewee
text: --is a diary during this time. I was always-- every girl was writing a diary. And I was writing a diary since I knew how to write. But they-- they were lost. And then I was writing my diary all the time in-- in ghetto-- in the ghetto, and then even in Fliegerhorst, and even at the very beginning of Plaszow. But then I sent all this, well, diaries to my Polish friend who saved them with photographs for me for after the war. But in the concentration camp, later on in Auschwitz and all the others, you couldn't write a diary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, of course not, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: You had nothing. So then--

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- but you kept a notebook, didn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I got in-- in-- in Auschwitz, I got from my colleague in the work such a small, red notebook, so small that I could keep this in my hand. And then I wrote my poems. And then this is like as if my diary took a concise form of a poem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the-- as far as the diary is concerned, is there-- are there any aspects of it that are actually portions that were written during this period? In other words, are you saying that part that--

speaker: interviewee
text: All of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, no, but some of it was written afterwards. Some of it has dates on it that is-- that is-- are-- that is quite current.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. This is different. When I was transcribing my diary, then it came to me. Like, for example, with that Jozef Hollander, who told me I will survive, and I will this and this. At this point, I thought, this needs explanation. And in order not to mix with the diary, I give the date when I was transcribing my diary.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see

speaker: interviewee
text: And I wrote there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So the diary then covers-- you actually have the-- the words that you wrote--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --during what periods? The-- the--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. The ghetto, the-- the pre-ghetto period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just before, pre-ghetto, ghetto, and beginning of Plaszow concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: The beginning of Plaszow. And then it picks up again at what point?

speaker: interviewee
text: It doesn't picks anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: It ends--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then-- then my-- then I just-- it goes from one poem into another. And I am fitting in between what-- what is there. As a matter of fact, my diary is the only existing document who came out of the ghetto in Krakow, in Polish language, from the milieu of Polish Jewish intelligentsia. There is no other written there with dates, with everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does any-- does-- does any foundation library or institute have this-- this diary available now for their--

speaker: interviewee
text: This books?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This-- that diary was published by Polish Cultural Foundation in Toronto. As a matter of fact, it was not published by the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know why. But when I sent-- transcribed this diary and I sent it to Yad Vashem, later on it was sent to a competition of Jews from Krakow in Tel Aviv. And because this is the only document, well, not written in retrospect-- like was written about 40 years later in retrospect from the memories of the people who were there. But my diary was really written there. So this diary got an award on this competition there. And that introduction to this diary was written by Gideon Hausner, who was the prosecutor of-- of Eichmann in Jerusalem. And then it was supposed to be published there. But they did not have money here. There was also, well, I don't know. But anyway, the Polish Cultural Foundation found out about this. And they wanted to publish. And they published it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- on what date and what--

speaker: interviewee
text: They published this, I think, 1987.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We can--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's out of print.

speaker: interviewer
text: We can refer back to this.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this books has-- well, maybe I-- I still have this main book of my diary with cover, which I make sketch, my drawings about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which you will-- we will photograph--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --later.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope. And there was that motto that I choose for my diary, that if evil will try to get to me, lift your spirits so high that it could not reach you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this was originally written by what little-known poet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zaleski.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: A romantic. I don't even remember. I just know Zaleski.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he-- he was Polish, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, Polish romantic poet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this statement has continued to be important to you throughout your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what ways?

speaker: interviewee
text: In many ways, because there is always evil somewhere. [LAUGHS] But when you just look at this and don't let this evil destroy you, maybe psychologically is more important than even physically.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as we began to talk now about your experiences in the camps--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --these words were always with you, [INAUDIBLE] words.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's take these words as we move, again, back into Fliegerhorst.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in that, as I mentioned, in the-- the Fliegerhorst--

speaker: interviewer
text: First, let's just make sure we've got this straight here. This was-- the date you arrived was December 1942, is  that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were there until?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] December 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the type of camp-- this camp was characterized as a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --the term for it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- there were two. There were different camps on the Fliegerhorst. It was some was [NON-ENGLISH]. One was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, the term for forced labor. Was there a term arbeits--

speaker: interviewee
text: Arbeitslager.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- is that-- is that how you would best characterize this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was arbeitslager. And we were-- and but they, later on when Plaszow was already there, we become [NON-ENGLISH] of Plaszow. It means that we administratively belong to Plaszow. And then Plaszow, they, one day people from Plaszow came. And [INAUDIBLE] [? people ?] Amon Goeth, the commandant of Plaszow, came. And they said that since we belong to them, we have to-- we have to be ruled by the same rules. And therefore, when people in Plaszow were taken, all that jewelry and this and that, so should we give everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, now, are we in Plaszow now?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are still in Fliegerhorst.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, while you were in Fliegerhorst, were you-- at first, you were working in Fliegerhorst while you were still in the ghetto, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then you moved from the ghetto to live on the base, the Luftwaffe base?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. We had barracks for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a-- it was a-- it was-- was this-- this was a Luftwaffe camp, which was a part of Plaszow then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So now you are living--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of thee--

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Fliegerhorst.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Fliegerhorst.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So continuing on from what you said about Fliegerhorst before, is there anything we need to do to begin to fill out this picture in terms of how you're able to survive, for instance, through this experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were working. Of course, we were working and we-- with all menial works. Sometimes when it just happened, partially in an office doing files or this or that. But-- but actually, it was physical work, except that we were not-- well, there was know that they come and beat us up and this-- this, like in-- we heard terrible stories about that liquidation of ghetto in Krakow. And-- and then when our-- my parents went to Plaszow--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, terrible stories in what form? I mean, what do you mean terrible?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we heard that they are-- we knew that when they-- the German are doing some kind of an action or transportation or deportation, there is always killing, stealing, beating, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any forms of resistance in Fliegerhorst?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I know. And I think it wasn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Compared to the experiences in the other camps, you would consider this experience--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- well, they were more or less-- at least they were not so hateful toward us. We were lucky, all of us. As a matter of fact, the largest percentage of people who survived from Krakow, they were this one who were, at this worst time, in Fliegerhorst, and not in-- in the ghetto in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in terms of your-- your way of life, so to speak, in Fliegerhorst, what were the living conditions like in those barracks, so to speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the barracks, where-- we were the girls'-- womans' barracks. We had rooms. There were about 20 girls in one room. They were double [INAUDIBLE] we have. Well, it was like a YMCA summer camp, one could say--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you sleeping on?

speaker: interviewee
text: --the barracks. Well, we were sleeping on the double beds. Well, we had--

speaker: interviewer
text: With mattresses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, straw mattresses. We had our own from home, places, beddings, this and that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so they let you take a certain amount of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, of course, of course. This was so that we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: So whatever belongings you had in the ghetto, you were allowed to take with you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were allowed--

speaker: interviewer
text: --completely.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to take. Yeah, sure, but depending how much space there is. But then when-- when the-- get camp from Plaszow to Fliegerhorst, they painted our-- if you know, everything, dresses, like stripes, yellow stripes this--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is in Fliegerhorst.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came later on when we become part of-- part of Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, OK. That hasn't happened yet. So let's stay--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: In terms of the-- but it  was certainly forced labor going on there. How many hours--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's correct. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This was usually we get up at 6 o'clock. And about 7 o'clock we got our-- I don't know, some breakfast or something. And before 8 o'clock--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which consisted of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bread and margarine or bread and cheese or bread and-- and sometimes sausage and-- and these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you'd work until when in the night?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, we work-- It depends. Usually some people, we came between-- from 8 o'clock between 12:00 and 1:00

speaker: interviewee
text: 00 and 2:00, we came for-- for soup back. And then back to work and about 6 o'clock.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: --you had free time in the evening then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so essentially, you were doing what type of work? You were-- you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything. Well, street cleaning. People were working in the washers. People were working with tailor shop, with, well, like--

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything related to the military, since it was a Luftwaffe camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were not-- not doing these things. We were just doing the menial work, scrubbing the floor, cleaning the windows, working on the [NON-ENGLISH]. Well, these kind of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody doing any administrative work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, sometimes. I even did also this for quite a few months, doing files for exchange part for the airplanes in the magazine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any instance of anyone working in an administrative capacity who might have been able to contribute to the resistance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's move, then, to Fliegerhorst as it becomes a part of Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only difference was like this that we were being painted here--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- your-- your--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and here, everything--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not moved.

speaker: interviewee
text: --clothes, dresses.

speaker: interviewer
text: You stayed in the same barracks, in other words, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we stayed there. And here, big number.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Fliegerhorst became sort of administratively taken over by--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the Plaszow administration.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes,that's right. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the-- but the location was the same. And in terms of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was--

speaker: interviewer
text: The nature of the work?

speaker: interviewee
text: --work was the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in terms of dehumanization, you notice--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Well, as long we were under-- under-- with that Air Force, it was different. It depends. Maybe someone was really terrible, well, depending on the human character.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. So this began, then, when-- when Fliegerhorst was administratively taken over by the Plaszow administration. That was in December 1943. It was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did that-- you remained in Plaszow, in that context--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. 1943, there was the end of this. Because they said that-- in-- in-- about six months earlier, we were taken so that we belonged to Fliegerhorst.

speaker: interviewer
text: I thought you said that it ended in Fliegerhorst in December 1943.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then they closed--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the camp and took us to Plaszow, all of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Oh, I see. So-- so Fliegerhorst became a part of Plaszow before December '43.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were still there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Essentially, you're talking about the two things in the same context.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's really--

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- but then we were still at Fliegerhorst when Fliegerhorst was taken over administratively--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. But--

speaker: interviewee
text: --by this. But we were still in Fliegerhorst.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in December, they closed the-- the camp and took us all to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Plaszow proper now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's December 1943. And you remained there until when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there maybe for about a month, when I was taken to another camp, also of the Air Force.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's maybe talk about Plaszow for a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrived in December 1943. And you stayed there until, what, January or February? Is that what you're saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you remember whether-- was it-- was it February? Was it January?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. But in terms of the type of camp, what would you call Plaszow? How would you have characterized it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plaszow scared me to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had absolutely no idea about. I was when the moment I went there with the-- with these wires, with-- with that electric wires, with the terrible noises, with that terrible language being used there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Terrible noises coming from what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Noise. Everybody-- well, you see, a sign of power it was that people were not talking, but yelling. And everybody was yelling.

speaker: interviewer
text: A sign of power?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meaning?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was when you have power in camp, well, then you yell.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the yelling was coming from who, though, in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: From everybody who wanted to be a powerful person. From the [NON-ENGLISH], from the [NON-ENGLISH], from every block [NON-ENGLISH], from every Jewish policeman, from every couple, from everyone there, there was-- there was noise. And there was the most dirty language possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you transported from Fliegerhorst to Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: By trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: By trucks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in each truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Maybe there were four trucks for all of us--

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were--

speaker: interviewee
text: --big trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: --individual trucks. And how long was that journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, well, 3/4 of an hour, half an hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And it-- OK. And-- OK. I think we better just switch tapes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to tell--
